problem_summary: 2012 MCM Problem B ("The Big Long River"): schedule white-water rafting trips on a 225-mile river with Y evenly-spaced campsites over a 6-month (180-day) season. Trips last 6-18 days, use either oar-propelled or motor rafts, and each campsite holds at most one party per night (no two groups share a site). The team must maximize the number of trips the company can run while preserving visitor satisfaction, and determine the river's "carrying capacity" — the max number of trips the system can support per season. This paper (Control #15878) models the river as a one-dimensional cellular-automaton-like array and simulates daily scheduling.
models_used: One-dimensional cellular-automaton-like simulation: river = array of Y cells (campsites), each cell either occupied by exactly one visitor object or empty; one iteration = one day; 180-day season

Agent/object-based 'visitor object' carrying state: raft type (oar 3-5 mph / motor 3-10 mph), anticipated trip length D_a, current position y, next-move distance y*, days traveled D_i, days remaining D_r, bump coefficient beta, and a bump direction (upstream/downstream)

Self-correcting ideal-move rule: each day recompute y* = (Y - y)/D_r (remaining distance divided by remaining days), rounded up to a discrete campsite count, clamped to the raft's min/max daily travel range from Table 1

Bump-coefficient conflict-resolution heuristic: beta = (Y - y)/D_r; on a campsite collision the visitor with the highest beta (most behind / most urgent) keeps the site, others search for nearest free site within move range in their bump direction, then the opposite direction, else are rejected
Reversible/randomized bump-direction rule: each visitor gets a random initial bump direction that flips every time it is bumped, to avoid being pushed twice the same way and drifting off schedule
Poisson arrival process: daily new-visitor demand generated with a Poisson(lambda) distribution, lambda swept from 1 to 15

Carrying-capacity definition as a constrained optimization over (Y, lambda): maximize completed trips subject to <10% rejections, <10% off-schedule completions, and <10 boat-to-boat interactions per person per day

Interaction model treating river motion as a state function: a raft-to-raft interaction is counted iff one party starts upstream of another and ends downstream of it that day (depends only on start/end positions); raft-to-camp and camp-to-camp interactions argued to be ~0 from assumptions
Environmental degradation extension: usable campsites reduced to floor(Y - alpha*log(lambda)) to model load-dependent site degradation

Storm/disruption extension: a stochastic event with fixed daily probability and Poisson(mu) duration that freezes movement and loading for N days while incrementing each visitor's days-traveled D_i (pushing them off schedule)
key_techniques: Recasting a continuous scheduling problem into a discrete cellular-automaton lattice so 'no two parties share a campsite' becomes the simple invariant 'one visitor per cell'
The bump coefficient beta = (Y - y)/D_r as a single scalar priority that fuses 'how far left to go' with 'how many days left' — equals the ideal pace, so the most-behind party wins ties

Designing controlled ablation experiments (Implementations A-G): toggling y* recalculation, bump rule on/off, and bump-direction policy to isolate the contribution of each mechanism rather than asserting the full model is best

Two-phase parameter sweep over (Y, lambda) visualized as 3D surface + contour plots for each metric (interactions, % rejected, % off-schedule, completions), turning carrying capacity into a readable response surface

Operationalizing the vague brief term 'visitor satisfaction' into three measurable, literature-grounded metrics (boat interactions, off-schedule completions, rejections) tied to social-norm carrying-capacity theory (Stankey, Vaske)
Distinguishing 'absolute capacity' (180Y) from realistic 'carrying capacity' with a memorable analogy (people squashed into every inch of a house) to justify a constrained definition
Static-vs-dynamic strategy framing: explicitly contrasting the industry-standard fixed-trip static schedule against their flexible dynamic recompute-daily scheduler, with a comparison table
why_it_won: "From a judge's view this paper wins because it nails the four things judges reward most. (1) A clean, well-chosen abstraction: the 1-D cellular-automaton array makes the hard constraint (one party per campsite) trivial to enforce and the model genuinely easy to implement and explain — it matches the problem's discrete structure exactly. (2) A single elegant idea that does the heavy lifting: the bump coefficient beta = (Y-y)/D_r is both the move rule and the conflict-priority rule, which is the kind of compact, defensible insight judges remember. (3) Rigorous self-criticism via control implementations A-G — instead of claiming their model is good, they prove which features matter (bumping gives ~400% more visitors; recalculating y* nearly eliminates off-schedule arrivals; bidirectional bumping beats unidirectional), which reads as scientific honesty. (4) A concrete, quantified answer to the actual question (carrying capacity = 1102 visitors/season at Y=180, lambda=15, with 98.2% accommodated, 99.3% on-time, conflicts eliminated) plus a practical, cost-aware caveat that Y=180 sites are expensive so Y=100-110 gives ~1025-1074 at far lower cost. It also extends past the basic ask with real-world stressors (environmental degradation and storms) and closes with a clear management memo — exactly the applied, communicative package MCM judges want."
section_highlights: "Structure flows in a textbook MCM arc and is unusually well-signposted. The 'Plan of Attack' (Sec 2) front-loads the roadmap. 'Assumptions' (Sec 3) are each stated with a justification (literature, industry practice, safety) rather than bare bullets. 'Important Definitions' (Sec 4) and 'Defining a Good Model' (Sec 5) set evaluation criteria BEFORE building the model, so results can be judged against pre-committed standards — a strong rhetorical move. The static-vs-dynamic comparison (Sec 6) with Table 2 motivates the design choice convincingly. The model section (Sec 7) layers cleanly: the visitor object's state variables, then generalized rules (Queue->Load->Move->Resolve Conflicts->Calculate, iterated 180 days, shown in flowcharts), then the specific ideal implementation, then the control variants in a tidy capability table (Table 3). Results (Sec 9) lead with the ablation tables (4 and 5) before the (Y,lambda) surface/contour plots, building the case step by step. 'Further Investigations' (Sec 10) stress-tests assumptions. The 'Memo to Rafting Management' (Sec 12) translates everything into plain, decision-maker language. Notation (D_a, D_i, D_r, y, y*, beta) is consistent and defined once. The writing repeatedly ties choices back to cited recreation/wilderness literature, giving the satisfaction metrics external validity."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Parameter values are loosely calibrated: raft speeds, 4-8 hr rafting days, and the Poisson lambda range (1-15) come from a couple of web itineraries and safety pages, not the problem data; the headline numbers inherit this softness

The reported diagnostic tables (4 and 5) fix Y=100, lambda=5, but the claimed carrying capacity is at Y=180, lambda=15 — the ablation evidence is at a different operating point than the final answer, a mild internal inconsistency

The % rejected surface data (Fig 6 table) shows 0% across a wide Y/lambda range and the table is truncated with '...', so the supporting data dump looks incomplete/garbled in the corpus and is hard to verify

Raft-to-camp and camp-to-camp interactions are simply assumed to be exactly zero (sites far from bank, campers don't hike far). This conveniently zeroes out two of the three interaction modes the satisfaction theory defines, weakening the satisfaction claim

One visitor 'object' = one party occupying one cell ignores party size and per-site capacity variation; campsites are treated as identical unit-capacity cells, so a real campground holding multiple/larger groups isn't represented

The bump heuristic is greedy and not proven optimal or near-optimal — no comparison to an exact ILP/optimization baseline, so 'ideal implementation' means best-among-our-variants, not provably optimal

The model never explicitly handles the trip-length DEMAND mix (6-18 day, oar vs motor proportions) as a decision variable; trips are randomly generated rather than the company actively choosing a trip menu, so the 'mix of trips to offer' part of the prompt is addressed only implicitly

Numbers are slightly inconsistent across the paper (carrying capacity quoted as 1102 in abstract/results but 1102.8 and a 'practical' 1074 in the conclusion; 1025 vs 1074 at Y=100/110), suggesting light editing slip
No sensitivity/robustness analysis on the move-distance clamping or on rounding y* up vs down; the discretization rule could materially shift interaction counts
reusable_patterns: Map a discrete scheduling/packing problem onto a cellular-automaton lattice (cells = scarce resources, agents = jobs, one iteration = one time step) to make capacity/conflict constraints into trivial occupancy invariants

Encode each agent as a state-carrying object and derive a single scalar priority (like beta = remaining-distance / remaining-time = required pace) that simultaneously drives movement AND breaks ties — one formula doing double duty is high-leverage and explainable
Use a self-correcting recompute-every-step rule (recalculate the required pace each iteration) instead of a fixed plan, to keep agents on schedule under perturbation

Build deliberate ablation/control variants of your own model (toggle each feature on/off) and report a table proving each component's marginal contribution — this is the strongest way to demonstrate a model is good rather than asserting it
Translate a subjective objective ('satisfaction') into 2-4 measurable, literature-cited metrics and pre-commit to threshold-based success criteria before showing results

Define the headline quantity (carrying capacity) as a constrained optimization: maximize throughput subject to all satisfaction metrics staying under explicit caps, then locate it on a (param1, param2) response surface visualized with 3D surface + contour plots

Separate 'absolute capacity' from 'realistic capacity' and report a cost-aware practical operating point, not just the theoretical maximum — judges and clients value the economically sensible recommendation

Stress-test by breaking your own assumptions in a 'Further Investigations' section (here: environmental degradation via floor(Y - alpha*log lambda) and storms via Poisson-duration freeze events) to show real-world robustness
Model exogenous random demand and disruptions with Poisson processes (arrivals with lambda, event durations with mu) for a simple, defensible stochastic layer
Close with a plain-language decision-maker memo that states the number, the operating conditions, and the trade-offs — the communication deliverable judges explicitly grade
